article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Work pace: F=11.816, Sig.=0.001 (COVID care 59.08 vs Not 49.78)
textual_interpretation: Work pace was significantly higher among those participating in COVID care (59.08 versus 49.78)
notes: Table 1, Between Groups ANOVA, N=268 (156 COVID, 112 non-COVID)

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Influence at work: F=25.855, Sig.<0.001 (COVID care 38.18 vs Not 51.79)
textual_interpretation: Those who participated in COVID care had significantly lower scores for influence at work (38.18 versus 51.79)

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Stress regression model: R-square=0.405, F=44.79, Sign.(p)<0.001
textual_interpretation: Stepwise multiple regression model explained 40.5% of the variance for stress
notes: Table 5, Regression F-test for overall stress model, predictors: work-family conflict, workplace commitment, emotional demands, competence transgression

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Work-family conflict -> Stress: B=0.32, Beta=0.421, t=8.079, p<0.001
textual_interpretation: Work-family conflict was a significant positive predictor of stress (Beta=0.421)

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Workplace commitment -> Stress: B=-0.2, Beta=-0.232, t=-4.712, p<0.001
textual_interpretation: Workplace commitment had a significant negative effect on stress (the more committed, the less stress)

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Emotional demands -> Stress: B=0.244, Beta=0.201, t=3.878, p<0.001
textual_interpretation: Emotional demands was a significant positive predictor of stress (Beta=0.201)

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Competence transgression -> Stress: B=3.039, Beta=0.108, t=2.181, p=0.030
textual_interpretation: Competence transgression had a significant positive effect on stress and exhibited the strongest linear relationship with stress

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Burnout regression model: R-square=0.398, F=43.524, Sign.(p)<0.001
textual_interpretation: Stepwise multiple regression model explained 39.8% of the variance for burnout
notes: Table 5, Regression F-test for overall burnout model, predictors: work-family conflict, workplace commitment, emotional demands, psychiatrist

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Work-family conflict -> Burnout: B=0.373, Beta=0.449, t=8.554, p<0.001
textual_interpretation: Work-family conflict was a significant positive predictor of burnout (Beta=0.449)

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Workplace commitment -> Burnout: B=-0.209, Beta=-0.222, t=-4.607, p<0.001
textual_interpretation: Workplace commitment was negatively associated with burnout (being committed to one's job was associated with lower burnout)

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Emotional demands -> Burnout: B=0.294, Beta=0.222, t=4.265, p<0.001
textual_interpretation: Emotional demands was a significant positive predictor of burnout (Beta=0.222)

article_title: Stress and burnout in the context of workplace psychosocial factors among mental health professionals during the later waves of the COVID-19 pandemic in Hungary
raw_text: Psychiatrist -> Burnout: B=-7.959, Beta=-0.155, t=-3.187, p=0.002
textual_interpretation: Being a psychiatrist was negatively associated with burnout (being a psychiatrist was associated with lower burnout scores)
notes: Table 5, Stepwise regression coefficient, dummy variable
